facts.text: The first feature-length motion picture made in Hollywood was 'The Squaw Man' (1914), directed by Cecil B. DeMille.
Charlie Chaplin's iconic 'Little Tramp' character first appeared in 'Kid Auto Races at Venice' in 1914.
Buster Keaton performed all of his own stunts, including the famous falling house facade in 'Steamboat Bill, Jr.' (1928), where a two-ton wall fell around him with only inches of clearance.
Harold Lloyd's famous clock-hanging scene in 'Safety Last!' (1923) was performed without a stunt double, despite Lloyd missing a thumb and forefinger from a prop bomb accident.
The German Expressionist film 'The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari' (1920) is considered one of the first true horror films and featured wildly distorted, painted sets.
'Nosferatu' (1922) was an unauthorized adaptation of Bram Stoker's 'Dracula,' and a court ordered all copies destroyed — but several prints survived.
Fritz Lang's 'Metropolis' (1927) was one of the most expensive films of the silent era, costing approximately 5 million Reichsmarks and using over 37,000 extras.
The original uncut version of 'Metropolis' was considered lost for decades until a nearly complete print was discovered in Buenos Aires in 2008.
'The Birth of a Nation' (1915) by D.W. Griffith was a landmark in filmmaking technique but remains deeply controversial for its racist content and glorification of the Ku Klux Klan.
The first Academy Awards ceremony was held on May 16, 1929, at the Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel, lasting only 15 minutes.
Wings (1927) won the first Best Picture Oscar at that inaugural ceremony, and it remains the only fully silent film to win the award.
Charlie Chaplin composed the music for many of his own films, including the famous smile theme from 'Modern Times' (1936).
'The Jazz Singer' (1927) is often cited as the first 'talkie,' though it was actually a mostly silent film with synchronized musical and dialogue sequences.
The transition from silent films to talkies in the late 1920s ended the careers of many silent film stars whose voices didn't match audience expectations.
Warner Bros. pioneered the Vitaphone sound-on-disc system that made 'The Jazz Singer' possible, betting the studio's future on sound technology.
Charlie Chaplin resisted the transition to talkies longer than most, releasing 'City Lights' (1931) as a silent film to great success.
Greta Garbo's first talkie, 'Anna Christie' (1930), was marketed with the tagline 'Garbo Talks!' — one of the most famous promotional lines in film history.
The Hollywood studio system of the 1930s-1950s operated on a 'vertical integration' model where studios owned production, distribution, and exhibition (theaters).
Under the studio system, actors were bound by long-term exclusive contracts, often lasting seven years, giving studios enormous control over their careers.
The 'Big Five' studios during Hollywood's Golden Age were MGM, Paramount, Warner Bros., 20th Century Fox, and RKO.
MGM's motto 'More Stars Than There Are in Heaven' reflected its roster that included Clark Gable, Judy Garland, Spencer Tracy, and many others.
The Hays Code (Motion Picture Production Code) was enforced from 1934 to 1968, strictly censoring content in American films.
Under the Hays Code, married couples in films had to be shown sleeping in separate beds, and kisses could not last longer than three seconds.
The Hays Code prohibited showing 'miscegenation' (interracial relationships), profanity, nudity, and any depiction of clergy as villains or comic figures.
Pre-Code Hollywood (1930-1934) produced surprisingly risqué films before the Hays Code was strictly enforced, including Mae West's provocative comedies.

'Gone with the Wind' (1939) famously included the line 'Frankly, my dear, I don't give a damn,' which required special permission under the Hays Code and resulted in a $5,000 fine for producer David O. Selznick.
'Gone with the Wind' premiered in Atlanta on December 15, 1939, and became the highest-grossing film of all time when adjusted for inflation.
Hattie McDaniel became the first African American to win an Academy Award for her role as Mammy in 'Gone with the Wind' (1940), but was seated at a segregated table at the ceremony.
Clark Gable was known as the 'King of Hollywood' and his removal of his undershirt in 'It Happened One Night' (1934) reportedly caused a significant drop in undershirt sales.
'Citizen Kane' (1941) was Orson Welles's debut feature film, made when he was only 25 years old, and is frequently cited as the greatest film ever made.
Orson Welles co-wrote, directed, produced, and starred in 'Citizen Kane,' pioneering deep-focus cinematography with cameraman Gregg Toland.
William Randolph Hearst, the newspaper magnate who inspired 'Citizen Kane,' tried to suppress the film by pressuring theaters not to show it and banning any mention of it in his publications.
'Rosebud,' the famous mystery word in 'Citizen Kane,' was reportedly a private joke — Hearst's pet name for his mistress Marion Davies.
'Casablanca' (1942) was based on an unproduced play called 'Everybody Comes to Rick's' and was considered a routine production, not expected to become a classic.
The line 'Here's looking at you, kid' in 'Casablanca' was improvised by Humphrey Bogart, who reportedly said it to Ingrid Bergman while teaching her poker between takes.
Contrary to popular belief, the exact line 'Play it again, Sam' is never spoken in 'Casablanca.' Ingrid Bergman says 'Play it, Sam' and Bogart says 'Play it!'
'Casablanca' won Best Picture at the 16th Academy Awards, held on March 2, 1944.
The Epstein twins (Julius and Philip) wrote much of the witty dialogue in 'Casablanca,' contributing lines that have become some of cinema's most quoted.
'The Wizard of Oz' (1939) was a box office disappointment on its initial release, only becoming a beloved classic through television broadcasts starting in 1956.
The ruby slippers in 'The Wizard of Oz' were originally silver in L. Frank Baum's book but were changed to ruby red to take advantage of the new Technicolor process.
Buddy Ebsen was originally cast as the Tin Man in 'The Wizard of Oz' but had to be replaced after suffering a severe allergic reaction to the aluminum powder makeup.
Multiple directors worked on 'The Wizard of Oz,' including Victor Fleming (who left to finish 'Gone with the Wind'), King Vidor, and others.
'Over the Rainbow' almost didn't make it into 'The Wizard of Oz' — MGM executives wanted to cut it, thinking it slowed down the film.
Technicolor's three-strip process, introduced in the mid-1930s, revolutionized cinema by producing rich, vibrant colors previously impossible on screen.
The first feature film to use the full three-strip Technicolor process was 'Becky Sharp' (1935).
Technicolor was so expensive that studios carefully rationed its use; many classic films of the 1930s and 1940s were still shot in black and white for cost reasons.
Natalie Kalmus, ex-wife of Technicolor inventor Herbert Kalmus, served as a 'color consultant' on nearly every Technicolor film, enforcing strict color guidelines.
'It's a Wonderful Life' (1946) was a box office flop when first released, losing money for its studio and nearly ending Frank Capra's career.
'It's a Wonderful Life' entered the public domain in 1974 due to a copyright renewal oversight, allowing TV stations to broadcast it freely, which turned it into a Christmas classic.
James Stewart considered his role as George Bailey in 'It's a Wonderful Life' his personal favorite performance.
Humphrey Bogart's career spanned from the early talkies through the 1950s, with his role in 'The African Queen' (1951) finally winning him his only Academy Award.
Bogart and Lauren Bacall's electric chemistry began on the set of 'To Have and Have Not' (1944); they married in 1945 and remained together until his death in 1957.
Humphrey Bogart was named the greatest male star in American film history by the American Film Institute in 1999.
Film noir emerged in the 1940s, characterized by cynical heroes, femmes fatales, shadowy cinematography, and morally ambiguous storylines.
The term 'film noir' (French for 'black film') was coined by French critic Nino Frank in 1946 to describe a new trend in American crime films.
'Double Indemnity' (1944), directed by Billy Wilder, is considered one of the defining films noir, with Barbara Stanwyck's Phyllis Dietrichson as the iconic femme fatale.
'The Maltese Falcon' (1941) was John Huston's directorial debut and Humphrey Bogart's breakout role as private detective Sam Spade.
'The Maltese Falcon' was actually the third film adaptation of Dashiell Hammett's novel, following versions in 1931 and 1936.
'Sunset Boulevard' (1950) starred Gloria Swanson as faded silent film star Norma Desmond, a role that eerily mirrored Swanson's own career trajectory.
The famous line 'All right, Mr. DeMille, I'm ready for my close-up' from 'Sunset Boulevard' is often misquoted as 'I'm ready for my close-up, Mr. DeMille.'
Billy Wilder originally wanted Mae West for the role of Norma Desmond in 'Sunset Boulevard,' but she turned it down, feeling the character was too old.
'The Third Man' (1949), set in post-war Vienna, features one of cinema's most famous entrances when Orson Welles's Harry Lime is revealed in a doorway by a cat.
The zither music score of 'The Third Man,' performed by Anton Karas, became an international hit and was the best-selling record in Britain for 11 weeks.
Orson Welles improvised the famous 'cuckoo clock' speech in 'The Third Man,' one of the most quoted monologues in film history.
'Touch of Evil' (1958) opens with one of cinema's most celebrated unbroken tracking shots, lasting over three minutes.
Alfred Hitchcock directed over 50 feature films in a career spanning six decades, earning the title 'Master of Suspense.'
Hitchcock made cameo appearances in 40 of his own films, a trademark that audiences eagerly watched for.
'Rear Window' (1954) was filmed entirely on a single elaborate set at Paramount Studios, one of the largest indoor sets ever built at the time.

Grace Kelly starred in three Hitchcock films — 'Dial M for Murder,' 'Rear Window,' and 'To Catch a Thief' — all within a two-year span before retiring from acting to marry Prince Rainier III of Monaco.
'Vertigo' (1958) was not a commercial success upon release but was later reassessed and voted the greatest film of all time in the 2012 Sight & Sound poll, dethroning 'Citizen Kane.'
The 'Vertigo effect' (dolly zoom) — zooming in while pulling the camera back — was invented for the film and has been used in countless movies since, including 'Jaws.'
'North by Northwest' (1959) features the iconic crop duster chase scene across empty farmland, one of the most imitated action sequences in cinema.
The Mount Rushmore finale of 'North by Northwest' was filmed on a studio set — the National Park Service refused permission to film on the actual monument.
Alfred Hitchcock's 'Psycho' (1960) was shot in black and white partly to reduce costs and partly so the famous shower scene's blood (actually chocolate syrup) wouldn't look too graphic.
'Psycho' premiered on June 16, 1960, and Hitchcock demanded that theaters not admit anyone after the film started — a revolutionary marketing strategy.
Janet Leigh, star of 'Psycho,' later said the shower scene affected her so deeply that she only took baths for the rest of her life.
The shower scene in 'Psycho' took seven days to shoot and contains about 70 camera angles in 45 seconds of screen time.
'The Birds' (1963) used a combination of real trained birds, mechanical birds, and innovative optical effects to create its terrifying avian attacks.
Hitchcock never won an Academy Award for Best Director, despite five nominations, one of the most notable oversights in Oscar history.
Audrey Hepburn won the Best Actress Oscar for her very first major American film, 'Roman Holiday' (1953), beating out established stars.
Audrey Hepburn's iconic little black dress in 'Breakfast at Tiffany's' (1961), designed by Givenchy, sold at auction for over $900,000 in 2006.
Truman Capote wanted Marilyn Monroe to play Holly Golightly in 'Breakfast at Tiffany's,' and was reportedly furious when Audrey Hepburn was cast instead.
Marilyn Monroe's famous white dress scene in 'The Seven Year Itch' (1955), filmed over a subway grate, reportedly contributed to the end of her marriage to Joe DiMaggio.
Marilyn Monroe studied at the Actors Studio under Lee Strasberg and was far more serious about her craft than her public image suggested.
Marilyn Monroe's last completed film was 'The Misfits' (1961), co-starring Clark Gable; it was also Gable's final film — he died shortly after filming.
James Dean completed only three films — 'East of Eden,' 'Rebel Without a Cause,' and 'Giant' — before his death in a car accident at age 24.
James Dean was the first actor to receive a posthumous Academy Award nomination, receiving two — for 'East of Eden' (1955) and 'Giant' (1956).
Cary Grant was born Archibald Alexander Leach in Bristol, England, reinventing himself as the epitome of Hollywood sophistication.
Cary Grant never won a competitive Academy Award, though he received an honorary Oscar in 1970 for his cumulative contribution to cinema.
Grace Kelly retired from acting at 26 to become Princess of Monaco, one of the most dramatic career changes in Hollywood history.
Elizabeth Taylor became the highest-paid actress in history when she received $1 million for 'Cleopatra' (1963) — the first time any actor had been paid that sum.
The production of 'Cleopatra' (1963) nearly bankrupted 20th Century Fox, with costs ballooning from $2 million to $44 million — equivalent to over $400 million today.
Elizabeth Taylor won two consecutive Best Actress Oscars — for 'BUtterfield 8' (1960) and 'Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?' (1966).
Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton's tempestuous romance during the filming of 'Cleopatra' was one of the biggest scandals of 1960s Hollywood.

Katharine Hepburn holds the record for most Best Actress Academy Awards with four wins: 'Morning Glory' (1933), 'Guess Who's Coming to Dinner' (1967), 'The Lion in Winter' (1968), and 'On Golden Pond' (1981).
Katharine Hepburn was once labeled 'box office poison' by theater exhibitors in 1938, only to make a triumphant comeback with 'The Philadelphia Story' (1940).
Spencer Tracy and Katharine Hepburn made nine films together over 25 years, while maintaining a private off-screen romance.
Spencer Tracy won back-to-back Best Actor Oscars for 'Captains Courageous' (1937) and 'Boys Town' (1938), one of only two actors to achieve this.
Clark Gable won the Academy Award for Best Actor for 'It Happened One Night' (1934), one of only two actors to win for a comedy performance in the early Oscar era.
Clark Gable's final film 'The Misfits' (1961) was also Marilyn Monroe's last completed film, making it a poignant bookend for two Hollywood eras.
John Ford won a record four Academy Awards for Best Director: 'The Informer' (1935), 'The Grapes of Wrath' (1940), 'How Green Was My Valley' (1941), and 'The Quiet Man' (1952).
John Ford's 'The Searchers' (1956) has been called the most influential film in American cinema, inspiring directors from Spielberg to Scorsese.
John Ford shot many of his greatest westerns in Monument Valley, making the Arizona landscape one of the most recognizable locations in cinema.
Cecil B. DeMille's 'The Ten Commandments' (1956) was one of the most expensive films ever made at the time and remained in the top ten highest-grossing films for decades.
For the original 'The Ten Commandments' (1923), DeMille built massive sets in the California desert that were simply buried when filming ended — they were rediscovered in the 1980s.
Billy Wilder, born in Austria-Hungary, fled Nazi Europe and became one of Hollywood's greatest director-writers, helming classics like 'Some Like It Hot,' 'The Apartment,' and 'Sunset Boulevard.'
Billy Wilder's 'Some Like It Hot' (1959) was voted the funniest American film of all time by the American Film Institute.
Marilyn Monroe required 47 takes to correctly say 'It's me, Sugar' in 'Some Like It Hot,' frustrating Wilder so much he eventually pasted the line inside a drawer she opened in the scene.
'Some Like It Hot' ends with one of cinema's greatest closing lines: 'Well, nobody's perfect,' delivered by Joe E. Brown after learning Jack Lemmon is a man.
Billy Wilder won the Academy Award for Best Director twice: for 'The Lost Weekend' (1945) and 'The Apartment' (1960).
Orson Welles struggled to finance films throughout his career after 'Citizen Kane,' often acting in other directors' films to fund his own projects.
Orson Welles's unfinished projects include 'The Other Side of the Wind,' which was finally completed and released by Netflix in 2018, over 40 years after filming began.
'Singin' in the Rain' (1952) is considered the greatest movie musical ever made, yet it was originally just a vehicle to reuse songs from MGM's music catalog.
Gene Kelly performed the iconic title number of 'Singin' in the Rain' with a 103-degree fever, and the 'rain' was actually a mixture of water and milk to make it show up on camera.
Debbie Reynolds was only 19 and had no dance training when cast in 'Singin' in the Rain'; Gene Kelly rehearsed her so hard that she once hid under a piano crying.

Donald O'Connor's 'Make 'Em Laugh' number in 'Singin' in the Rain' was so physically demanding that he was hospitalized afterward and had to re-film it days later because the original footage was ruined.
'West Side Story' (1961) won 10 Academy Awards including Best Picture, the most ever for a musical at that time.
Natalie Wood's singing voice in 'West Side Story' was dubbed by Marni Nixon, who also dubbed Audrey Hepburn in 'My Fair Lady' and Deborah Kerr in 'The King and I.'
'My Fair Lady' (1964) controversially cast Audrey Hepburn instead of Julie Andrews, who had originated the role on Broadway — Andrews went on to win the Oscar that year for 'Mary Poppins' instead.
'The Sound of Music' (1965) became the highest-grossing film of all time upon release, surpassing 'Gone with the Wind' in raw box office terms.
The real Maria von Trapp made a cameo appearance in 'The Sound of Music,' walking through a scene in the background during 'I Have Confidence.'
'Mary Poppins' (1964) combined live action and animation in ways never before achieved, winning five Academy Awards including Best Actress for Julie Andrews.
Walt Disney pursued the rights to 'Mary Poppins' for 20 years before author P.L. Travers finally agreed — and she reportedly cried at the premiere because she disliked the result.
Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers made 10 films together at RKO, defining the movie musical partnership with their effortless grace and chemistry.
Fred Astaire's famous screen test report allegedly read: 'Can't act. Slightly bald. Can dance a little.' This is one of Hollywood's most famous underestimations.
Fred Astaire was such a perfectionist that he insisted on filming dance numbers in full-body, continuous shots so audiences could see the complete choreography.
The first drive-in movie theater opened on June 6, 1933, in Camden, New Jersey, charging 25 cents per car plus 25 cents per person.
At their peak in the late 1950s, there were over 4,000 drive-in theaters across the United States.
Drive-in theaters were sometimes called 'passion pits' because they offered privacy for young couples, a reputation that contributed to their popularity in the 1950s.
The Hollywood sign originally read 'HOLLYWOODLAND' when it was erected in 1923 as a real estate advertisement; the last four letters were removed in 1949.
'King Kong' (1933) was a groundbreaking special effects achievement, using stop-motion animation, rear projection, and miniatures to bring the giant ape to life.
The original 'King Kong' model was only 18 inches tall, yet Willis O'Brien's stop-motion animation made it seem colossal on screen.
'Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs' (1937) was the first full-length cel-animated feature film and was derisively dubbed 'Disney's Folly' before becoming a massive hit.
'Fantasia' (1940) was one of the first commercial films released in stereophonic sound, using a system called 'Fantasound.'
The Motion Picture Production Code (Hays Code) was replaced by the MPAA rating system on November 1, 1968, ushering in a new era of creative freedom.
The Supreme Court's 1948 Paramount Decision forced studios to divest their theater chains, effectively ending the studio system's monopoly.
Vivien Leigh was a relatively unknown British actress when she was cast as Scarlett O'Hara in 'Gone with the Wind' after a legendary two-year, 1,400-actress search.
Vivien Leigh won Best Actress Oscars for both 'Gone with the Wind' (1939) and 'A Streetcar Named Desire' (1951), remarkably for only her first and sixth American films.
Marlon Brando revolutionized screen acting with his Method performance in 'A Streetcar Named Desire' (1951), bringing raw naturalism to Hollywood.
Marlon Brando's screen test for 'Rebel Without a Cause' was passed on, and the role went to James Dean — a casting decision that defined 1950s youth culture.
Marlon Brando won the Oscar for 'On the Waterfront' (1954) and 'The Godfather' (1972), famously refusing the latter in protest of Hollywood's treatment of Native Americans.
'On the Waterfront' (1954) won eight Academy Awards, and Brando's 'I coulda been a contender' speech is among the most quoted in cinema.
'All About Eve' (1950) holds the record for the most Academy Award nominations for a single film with 14, a record it shared with 'Titanic' (1997).
Bette Davis and Joan Crawford's famous feud reached its peak during the filming of 'What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?' (1962), with each actress reportedly trying to sabotage the other.
Bette Davis was the first woman to receive 10 Academy Award nominations for acting.
Joan Crawford won her only Oscar for 'Mildred Pierce' (1945) but claimed she was too nervous to attend the ceremony and accepted from her bed, surrounded by press photographers.
'Rashomon' (1950) by Akira Kurosawa introduced the concept of multiple contradictory perspectives of the same event to Western audiences, influencing countless films.
Akira Kurosawa's 'Seven Samurai' (1954) has been remade and referenced in hundreds of films, most famously as 'The Magnificent Seven' (1960).
Italian neorealism, exemplified by films like 'Bicycle Thieves' (1948) and 'Rome, Open City' (1945), used non-professional actors and real locations, profoundly influencing world cinema.
Federico Fellini's '8½' (1963) is a semi-autobiographical film about a director struggling with creative block, and its title refers to the number of films Fellini had directed.
Ingmar Bergman's 'The Seventh Seal' (1957) features the iconic image of a knight playing chess with Death, one of cinema's most parodied and referenced scenes.
'The Bridge on the River Kwai' (1957) won seven Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Director for David Lean.
David Lean's 'Lawrence of Arabia' (1962) had no speaking female roles in its entire 227-minute runtime.
Peter O'Toole was an unknown stage actor when cast as T.E. Lawrence in 'Lawrence of Arabia'; the role made him an international star overnight.
'Ben-Hur' (1959) won a record 11 Academy Awards, a record that stood until 'Titanic' (1997) and 'The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King' (2003) tied it.
The chariot race in 'Ben-Hur' took five weeks to film on the largest set ever built at the time — an 18-acre arena in Rome's Cinecittà Studios.
Charlton Heston did most of his own chariot driving in 'Ben-Hur,' though a stunt double handled the most dangerous moments.

Walt Disney's 'Bambi' (1942) was so realistic in its animation of animals that the studio hired a Chinese artist, Tyrus Wong, whose minimalist watercolor backgrounds gave the film its distinctive look.
'Pinocchio' (1940) was a financial disaster on initial release due to World War II cutting off European markets, despite being considered one of Disney's greatest artistic achievements.
The first color feature film was 'The Toll of the Sea' (1922), using an early two-color Technicolor process.
'A Streetcar Named Desire' (1951) was heavily censored under the Hays Code, cutting key scenes that weren't restored until decades later.
The Hollywood blacklist of the late 1940s and 1950s destroyed careers of writers, directors, and actors accused of Communist sympathies.
The 'Hollywood Ten' — ten screenwriters and directors who refused to testify before the House Un-American Activities Committee — were jailed and blacklisted in 1947.
Dalton Trumbo, one of the Hollywood Ten, continued writing screenplays under pseudonyms during the blacklist, winning two Academy Awards under fake names.
Kirk Douglas helped break the Hollywood blacklist by giving Dalton Trumbo screen credit for 'Spartacus' (1960).
Sidney Poitier became the first Black actor to win the Academy Award for Best Actor for 'Lilies of the Field' (1963).
Dorothy Dandridge was the first African American woman nominated for the Academy Award for Best Actress, for 'Carmen Jones' (1954).
'12 Angry Men' (1957) takes place almost entirely in a single jury room, yet remains one of the most gripping and acclaimed films ever made.
Henry Fonda's performance as Juror #8 in '12 Angry Men' was unrehearsed on the first take to capture the genuine reactions of the other actors.
The widescreen format CinemaScope was introduced in 1953 with 'The Robe,' designed to lure audiences away from television.
3D films were first widely promoted in the 1950s, with 'Bwana Devil' (1952) launching a brief craze, though audiences tired of wearing the uncomfortable glasses.
Cinerama, using three synchronized projectors on a curved screen, debuted in 1952 as another attempt to offer an experience television couldn't match.
'Rebel Without a Cause' (1955) tackled teen angst and delinquency, making James Dean an icon of youthful rebellion overnight.
Three actors from 'Rebel Without a Cause' — James Dean, Sal Mineo, and Nick Adams — all died young under tragic circumstances, fueling legends about a 'curse.'
Gary Cooper's laconic 'Yup' delivery made him one of Hollywood's most distinctive actors; he won Best Actor Oscars for 'Sergeant York' (1941) and 'High Noon' (1952).
'High Noon' (1952) was filmed in real time, with clocks visible throughout to build tension as the noon showdown approaches.
'High Noon' was widely interpreted as an allegory for the Hollywood blacklist, with the townspeople representing those who wouldn't stand up against McCarthyism.
John Wayne called 'High Noon' 'the most un-American thing I've ever seen' due to its perceived criticism of American values.
John Wayne's real name was Marion Robert Morrison. He adopted his screen name at the suggestion of director Raoul Walsh.
John Wayne appeared in over 170 films and was the top box office draw for three decades, embodying American masculinity on screen.
'Stagecoach' (1939), directed by John Ford, made John Wayne a star after years of B-movie roles.
Judy Garland was only 16 when she filmed 'The Wizard of Oz' and was put on a strict diet and given pills by the studio, beginning a lifelong struggle with substance abuse.
Judy Garland's daughter, Liza Minnelli, would also become an Oscar-winning actress-singer, making them one of Hollywood's most legendary mother-daughter duos.
'A Star Is Born' (1954), starring Judy Garland, was cut by nearly 30 minutes after its premiere by Warner Bros., and the lost footage wasn't recovered for decades.
The original 'Frankenstein' (1931) starring Boris Karloff was so shocking that it included a spoken prologue warning audiences about the frightening content.
Boris Karloff's monster makeup in 'Frankenstein' was created by Jack Pierce and took four hours to apply each day of shooting.
Bela Lugosi turned down the role of Frankenstein's monster because the heavy makeup would hide his face; Boris Karloff took the role and became more famous as a result.
Universal Studios built its horror empire in the 1930s with 'Dracula,' 'Frankenstein,' 'The Mummy,' 'The Invisible Man,' and 'The Wolf Man.'
'Dracula' (1931) starring Bela Lugosi established many vampire tropes still used today, including the cape, accent, and hypnotic stare.
'Freaks' (1932) used real circus performers and was so controversial that it was banned in the UK for 30 years.
Walt Disney received a special Academy Award for 'Snow White' that consisted of one full-sized Oscar and seven miniature ones.
Shirley Temple was the top box office star in America from 1935 to 1938, earning more than most adult actors combined.
Shirley Temple received a special miniature Academy Award at age six in 1935, in recognition of her outstanding contribution to screen entertainment.

'It Happened One Night' (1934) was the first film to sweep the five major Academy Awards: Best Picture, Director, Actor, Actress, and Screenplay — a feat not repeated until 'One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest' (1975).
Claudette Colbert nearly turned down 'It Happened One Night,' only agreeing after Columbia doubled her salary and promised filming would take only four weeks.
'Grand Hotel' (1932) was the first film to feature an all-star cast, including Greta Garbo, John Barrymore, Joan Crawford, and Wallace Beery.
Greta Garbo retired from films at age 36 after the disappointing reception of 'Two-Faced Woman' (1941) and never made another movie, living as a recluse in New York.
Greta Garbo's famous line 'I want to be alone' from 'Grand Hotel' became so associated with her that she later said, 'I never said I want to be alone. I only said I want to be let alone.'
The Marx Brothers — Groucho, Harpo, Chico, and Zeppo — made 13 feature films, with 'Duck Soup' (1933) and 'A Night at the Opera' (1935) considered their masterpieces.
Groucho Marx's painted-on mustache, stooped walk, and rapid-fire wit made him one of the most imitated comedians in history.
W.C. Fields and Mae West were so popular and controversial that the Hays Code office specifically targeted their films for stricter enforcement.
Laurel and Hardy made over 100 films together, establishing the template for comedy duos that would influence everyone from Abbott and Costello to modern buddy comedies.
The Three Stooges appeared in 190 short comedy films for Columbia Pictures between 1934 and 1959, more than any other comedy act in film history.
'Bringing Up Baby' (1938), starring Cary Grant and Katharine Hepburn, was a box office failure that nearly ended both their careers but is now considered one of the greatest screwball comedies.
Screwball comedy emerged during the Great Depression, offering audiences fast-paced, witty escapism often centered on the battle of the sexes.
Preston Sturges became the first screenwriter to direct his own script when he made 'The Great McGinty' (1940), launching a remarkable run of comedy classics.
'Gaslight' (1944) starring Ingrid Bergman gave rise to the psychological term 'gaslighting,' meaning to manipulate someone into questioning their own sanity.

Ingrid Bergman was ostracized by Hollywood after leaving her husband for Italian director Roberto Rossellini in 1950, not returning to American films until 'Anastasia' (1956), for which she won an Oscar.

'Notorious' (1946) contains one of the longest kiss scenes in cinema history — Hitchcock got around the Hays Code's three-second rule by having Cary Grant and Ingrid Bergman kiss, talk, and kiss repeatedly.
'Rebecca' (1940) was Hitchcock's first American film and won the Academy Award for Best Picture.
Hitchcock's 'Rope' (1948) was designed to appear as one continuous take, using hidden cuts when the camera moved behind furniture or actors.
'Strangers on a Train' (1951) features one of Hitchcock's most chilling villains in Robert Walker's Bruno Anthony, who proposes a 'criss-cross' murder swap.
The carousel climax of 'Strangers on a Train' used a real out-of-control merry-go-round — a crew member crawled under the spinning ride to reach the controls and stop it, risking his life.
'Dial M for Murder' (1954) was filmed in 3D at Warner Bros.' insistence, though it was mostly shown flat; the 3D version is now considered superior.
William Wyler directed three Best Picture winners: 'Mrs. Miniver' (1942), 'The Best Years of Our Lives' (1946), and 'Ben-Hur' (1959).
'The Best Years of Our Lives' (1946) featured Harold Russell, a real World War II veteran who lost both hands, winning two Academy Awards — one for Best Supporting Actor and an honorary award.
Frank Capra's films, including 'Mr. Smith Goes to Washington' (1939) and 'It's a Wonderful Life' (1946), defined the 'Capra-corn' style of idealistic, feel-good Americana.
'Mr. Smith Goes to Washington' (1939) so angered the U.S. Senate that senators considered banning it; it was also banned in several European dictatorships.
Errol Flynn's swashbuckling roles in 'The Adventures of Robin Hood' (1938) and 'Captain Blood' (1935) made him the definitive action star of Hollywood's Golden Age.
'The Adventures of Robin Hood' (1938) was one of the first major films shot entirely in three-strip Technicolor, and its vibrant colors remain stunning today.
The original 'A Star Is Born' (1937) has been remade three times — in 1954 (Judy Garland), 1976 (Barbra Streisand), and 2018 (Lady Gaga).
'Wuthering Heights' (1939) made Laurence Olivier a Hollywood star, though he initially didn't want to do it and had to be convinced by director William Wyler.
Laurence Olivier's film adaptations of Shakespeare — 'Henry V' (1944), 'Hamlet' (1948), and 'Richard III' (1955) — remain among the finest Shakespeare films ever made.
'Hamlet' (1948) directed by and starring Laurence Olivier is the only Shakespeare film adaptation to win the Academy Award for Best Picture.
Orson Welles was only 25 when he arrived in Hollywood with a contract giving him final cut and complete creative control — an unprecedented deal for a first-time filmmaker.
RKO Pictures, the studio that produced 'Citizen Kane' and 'King Kong,' was the smallest of the Big Five and eventually ceased production in 1957 after being sold to Desilu Productions.
'The Philadelphia Story' (1940) rescued Katharine Hepburn's career — she shrewdly bought the film rights to the play, then sold them to MGM on the condition she star.
Cary Grant donated his entire salary from 'The Philadelphia Story' to the British War Relief Fund during World War II.
During World War II, Hollywood produced hundreds of propaganda and morale-boosting films, and major stars like Jimmy Stewart, Clark Gable, and Henry Fonda served in the military.
Jimmy Stewart flew 20 combat missions over Germany as a B-24 bomber pilot during World War II, earning the Distinguished Flying Cross.
After the war, Jimmy Stewart struggled with PTSD and credits his role in 'It's a Wonderful Life' with helping him process his experiences.
The USO shows during World War II brought Hollywood stars like Bob Hope, Marlene Dietrich, and the Andrews Sisters to entertain troops around the world.
Marlene Dietrich became an American citizen in 1939 and actively opposed the Nazis, entertaining Allied troops at personal risk throughout World War II.
'Casablanca' was rushed into release to coincide with the Allied invasion of North Africa in November 1942, giving it unexpected topical relevance.
Film noir's visual style was heavily influenced by German Expressionism, with many noir directors and cinematographers being European émigrés who fled the Nazis.
'The Big Sleep' (1946) has a plot so convoluted that not even author Raymond Chandler could explain who committed one of the murders when asked.
Lauren Bacall was only 19 when she starred opposite Humphrey Bogart in 'To Have and Have Not' (1944), and her sultry debut remains one of cinema's most iconic introductions.
'Laura' (1944) features one of the most memorable twists in film noir — the appearance of the supposedly murdered woman halfway through the film.
'The Postman Always Rings Twice' (1946) was the third attempt to film James M. Cain's novel — previous versions had been blocked by the Hays Code.
Robert Mitchum's hooded eyes and laid-back demeanor made him the quintessential noir anti-hero, memorably showcased in 'Out of the Past' (1947) and 'The Night of the Hunter' (1955).
'The Night of the Hunter' (1955) was the only film directed by actor Charles Laughton; it flopped commercially but is now considered a masterpiece of American cinema.
Robert Mitchum had 'LOVE' and 'HATE' tattooed on his knuckles in 'The Night of the Hunter,' a detail later referenced in numerous films including 'Do the Right Thing.'
Film stock used in early cinema was made of cellulose nitrate, which was highly flammable — this is why many silent films have been permanently lost in vault fires.
It is estimated that approximately 75% of all silent films are lost forever, destroyed by fire, decay, or intentional disposal by studios that didn't see value in preserving them.
The Library of Congress established the National Film Registry in 1988 to preserve films deemed 'culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant.'
Georges Méliès's 'A Trip to the Moon' (1902) is one of the earliest narrative films and features the iconic image of a rocket landing in the Moon's eye.
'The Great Train Robbery' (1903) is often considered the first Western and one of the earliest narrative films; its final shot of a cowboy firing at the camera shocked audiences.
D.W. Griffith pioneered many filmmaking techniques including the close-up, the fade-out, and parallel editing, fundamentally creating the language of cinema.
Erich von Stroheim's 'Greed' (1924) was originally 9 hours long; the studio cut it to 2 hours, and the excised footage is considered one of cinema's greatest losses.
'The Gold Rush' (1925) features Charlie Chaplin's famous bread roll dance, which he performed by sticking forks into two dinner rolls and making them 'dance' on a table.
Charlie Chaplin was so meticulous that the opening scene of 'City Lights' (1931) — where the Tramp is discovered sleeping on a statue — required 342 takes.
'Modern Times' (1936) was one of the last major silent films, as Chaplin used only music and sound effects with minimal dialogue.

Buster Keaton's 'The General' (1926), based on a true Civil War incident, featured one of the most expensive single shots in silent cinema: a locomotive crashing through a burning bridge into a river.
Harold Lloyd was one of the highest-grossing stars of the silent era, often out-earning both Chaplin and Keaton at the box office.
Louise Brooks, with her distinctive bob haircut, starred in the German Expressionist classic 'Pandora's Box' (1929) and became an enduring style icon.
Lon Chaney, 'The Man of a Thousand Faces,' performed his own extraordinary makeup transformations in films like 'The Phantom of the Opera' (1925) and 'The Hunchback of Notre Dame' (1923).
Mary Pickford, known as 'America's Sweetheart,' was one of the most powerful figures in early Hollywood, co-founding United Artists with Chaplin, Douglas Fairbanks, and D.W. Griffith in 1919.
Douglas Fairbanks was the first great action star, performing his own stunts in swashbucklers like 'The Mark of Zorro' (1920) and 'The Thief of Bagdad' (1924).
The first Academy Awards ceremony on May 16, 1929, had only 270 guests and the winners had been announced three months earlier.
'All Quiet on the Western Front' (1930) won Best Picture and was so anti-war that the Nazis banned it in Germany and released rats in theaters showing it.
'Mutiny on the Bounty' (1935) was one of only three films where three of its actors — Clark Gable, Charles Laughton, and Franchot Tone — all received Best Actor nominations.
'The Thin Man' (1934) spawned a series of six films starring William Powell and Myrna Loy, establishing the template for the witty married-couple mystery genre.
Myrna Loy was voted 'Queen of Hollywood' in a 1936 poll, beating out Garbo, Dietrich, and Crawford.
Orson Welles's radio broadcast of 'The War of the Worlds' on October 30, 1938, caused mass panic, demonstrating his dramatic genius before he ever made a film.
'The Grapes of Wrath' (1940) was praised by both conservatives and liberals upon release, an unusual achievement for a socially conscious film.
Henry Fonda considered Tom Joad in 'The Grapes of Wrath' his defining role and signed a seven-year contract with Fox specifically to play the part.
'Citizen Kane' pioneered the use of deep focus cinematography, where everything from the foreground to background is in sharp focus simultaneously.
The famous breakfast montage in 'Citizen Kane' condenses the deterioration of a marriage into a few minutes through increasingly distant seating and hostile dialogue.
'Bambi' (1942) was protested by hunters' groups, and the death of Bambi's mother remains one of the most emotional moments in film history.
During the 1940s, weekly movie attendance in the United States peaked at about 90 million people — roughly two-thirds of the total population.
Newsreels shown before feature films were the primary way Americans saw visual news coverage before television became widespread in the 1950s.
'Double Indemnity' was adapted by Billy Wilder and Raymond Chandler, who reportedly hated each other so much that Chandler once threw Wilder out of his office.
'Mildred Pierce' (1945) blended film noir with women's melodrama, creating a unique hybrid that showcased Joan Crawford's dramatic range.
Orson Welles's 'The Magnificent Ambersons' (1942) was taken from him by RKO and drastically re-edited, with the original ending destroyed — it's considered one of cinema's great tragedies.
'Brief Encounter' (1945), directed by David Lean, is considered one of the greatest romantic films ever made, telling the story of a doomed love affair between two married strangers.
Italian director Vittorio De Sica's 'Bicycle Thieves' (1948) used entirely non-professional actors and is a cornerstone of the neorealist movement.
'Treasure of the Sierra Madre' (1948) won John Huston the Oscar for Best Director, while his father Walter Huston won Best Supporting Actor — the first father-son double win.
'Sunset Boulevard' was so cutting in its depiction of Hollywood that Louis B. Mayer reportedly shouted at Billy Wilder, 'You have disgraced the industry that made and fed you!'
Gloria Swanson was herself a major silent film star, making her casting as faded star Norma Desmond in 'Sunset Boulevard' a masterful piece of meta-casting.
The dead body of William Holden's character narrates 'Sunset Boulevard' in flashback — a darkly innovative framing device that shocked 1950 audiences.
'An American in Paris' (1951) won Best Picture and featured a 17-minute ballet sequence set to Gershwin's music, one of the most ambitious dance numbers in film history.
Gene Kelly choreographed most of his own dance numbers and pushed the boundaries of what was possible in movie musicals with athletic, innovative routines.

'The African Queen' (1951) was filmed on location in the Belgian Congo and Uganda, with cast and crew suffering from dysentery — Humphrey Bogart and John Huston avoided it by drinking whiskey instead of water.
Humphrey Bogart died on January 14, 1957, at age 57. His wife Lauren Bacall placed a small gold whistle in his coffin — a reference to their first film together.
'Stalag 17' (1953), directed by Billy Wilder, was a precursor to shows like 'Hogan's Heroes' and won William Holden the Best Actor Oscar.
'From Here to Eternity' (1953) won eight Academy Awards and featured the famous beach kiss between Burt Lancaster and Deborah Kerr.
Montgomery Clift, one of the original Method actors, suffered a devastating car accident in 1956 that altered his appearance and career trajectory dramatically.
'On the Waterfront' (1954) was directed by Elia Kazan, who had controversially named names before the House Un-American Activities Committee, and many saw the film as his justification.
Grace Kelly won the Best Actress Oscar for 'The Country Girl' (1954), beating out Judy Garland in 'A Star Is Born' — one of the most debated upsets in Oscar history.
The VistaVision widescreen process was developed by Paramount and first used in 'White Christmas' (1954), running film horizontally through the camera for a sharper image.
'Guys and Dolls' (1955) starred Marlon Brando as Sky Masterson, though Brando could barely sing — Frank Sinatra, who could, was cast in the secondary role and was reportedly resentful.
James Dean's car, a Porsche 550 Spyder nicknamed 'Little Bastard,' has been the subject of numerous legends about a curse, with multiple people associated with it meeting misfortune.
'Giant' (1956) was James Dean's last film; he died before it was released and received a posthumous Oscar nomination for his performance.
Rock Hudson, one of the biggest box office stars of the 1950s and 1960s, kept his homosexuality secret throughout his career due to Hollywood and societal expectations.
'The Ten Commandments' (1956) used over 14,000 extras for the Exodus scene, one of the largest crowd scenes ever filmed without CGI.
Yul Brynner starred in 'The King and I' (1956), a role he played over 4,600 times on stage, and his shaved head became his trademark.
'Paths of Glory' (1957) was Stanley Kubrick's anti-war masterpiece; it was banned in France for nearly 20 years because of its portrayal of the French military.

Stanley Kubrick's 'Spartacus' (1960) was the director's only studio film where he didn't have full creative control — an experience that led him to insist on complete autonomy for all future projects.
'Vertigo' was poorly reviewed and commercially under-performed when released in 1958, and Hitchcock blamed Jimmy Stewart's age for the failure.
Kim Novak's dual role in 'Vertigo' required her to portray two seemingly different women, in what many consider one of the most complex performances in classic cinema.
'Some Like It Hot' was released without a Hays Code seal of approval because of its cross-dressing and sexual themes, yet it was hugely successful.
Tony Curtis said kissing Marilyn Monroe in 'Some Like It Hot' was 'like kissing Hitler,' a remark Monroe found deeply hurtful.
'North by Northwest's' famous crop duster scene was filmed near Bakersfield, California, and Cary Grant actually ran from the plane in several takes.
'Anatomy of a Murder' (1959) was one of the first mainstream films to use explicit language, with words like 'rape' and 'panties' causing controversy under the Hays Code.
'The Apartment' (1960) was the last black-and-white film to win the Academy Award for Best Picture until 'The Artist' (2011) over 50 years later.
Jack Lemmon and Billy Wilder collaborated on seven films, forming one of Hollywood's greatest director-actor partnerships.
'Breakfast at Tiffany's' (1961) featured Mickey Rooney in a controversial yellowface portrayal of Mr. Yunioshi, now widely considered one of Hollywood's most offensive performances.
The moon door scene from 'Breakfast at Tiffany's,' where Holly Golightly sings 'Moon River' on the fire escape, almost didn't make the final cut — a studio executive wanted it removed.
'The Hustler' (1961) starred Paul Newman as Fast Eddie Felson, a role he reprised 25 years later in 'The Color of Money' (1986), finally winning his first competitive Oscar.
'To Kill a Mockingbird' (1962) won Gregory Peck the Best Actress — Best Actor Oscar, and his portrayal of Atticus Finch was named the greatest movie hero of all time by the AFI.
Gregory Peck was so moved by the script of 'To Kill a Mockingbird' that he called producer Alan Pakula and said, 'If you want me, I'll do it,' without any negotiation.
'Lawrence of Arabia' used no women in speaking roles and no stock footage — every frame was shot original on location in Jordan, Morocco, and Spain.
Peter O'Toole received eight Best Actor nominations over his career without a single win, the most nominations without winning in any acting category.
'Dr. No' (1962), the first James Bond film, almost starred Cary Grant, but producers wanted an unknown who could commit to a series — Sean Connery got the role.
Sean Connery wore a toupee in every James Bond film, as he had started losing his hair in his twenties.
'The Manchurian Candidate' (1962) was pulled from release after President Kennedy's assassination and wasn't widely available again until 1988.
'The Birds' marked the first time Hitchcock didn't use a traditional musical score — the soundtrack consists entirely of electronically produced bird sounds.
'Cleopatra' (1963) began filming with one director (Rouben Mamoulian) and star (Peter Finch as Caesar) before both were replaced by Joseph L. Mankiewicz and Rex Harrison.
'The Great Escape' (1963) was based on a true story, but Steve McQueen's famous motorcycle chase was entirely fictional — the real escapees traveled by foot and train.
Steve McQueen became the 'King of Cool' in the 1960s, performing many of his own stunts, though a professional rider doubled for him in the most dangerous motorcycle jumps in 'The Great Escape.'
'Dr. Strangelove' (1964) was Stanley Kubrick's brilliant Cold War satire, in which Peter Sellers played three different roles.
Peter Sellers improvised much of 'Dr. Strangelove,' including the character's involuntary Nazi salute and his struggle to keep his mechanical hand from strangling himself.
'Goldfinger' (1964) established the Bond formula of gadgets, one-liners, and spectacular villains, and its painted-gold body was one of the decade's most iconic images.
The actress painted gold in 'Goldfinger,' Shirley Eaton, had a small patch of skin left unpainted because the filmmakers incorrectly believed a fully painted person would die from skin asphyxiation.
'Mary Poppins' (1964) was nominated for 13 Academy Awards, winning five, and its blend of live action and animation was groundbreaking for its time.
'A Hard Day's Night' (1964) the Beatles' debut film, was made for just £200,000 and helped invent the modern music video with its innovative visual style.
'My Fair Lady' (1964) cost $17 million, making it one of the most expensive films of its era, and won eight Academy Awards including Best Picture.
'The Sound of Music' was such a massive hit that it saved 20th Century Fox from bankruptcy after the disastrous losses from 'Cleopatra.'
Julie Andrews was simultaneously starring in 'Mary Poppins' and 'The Sound of Music,' winning the Oscar for the former and a nomination for the latter in consecutive years.
'Doctor Zhivago' (1965), directed by David Lean, was filmed primarily in Spain because the Soviet Union refused to cooperate with the production.
'Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?' (1966) was one of the first films to receive the MPAA's 'Suggested for Mature Audiences' label.
Richard Burton and Elizabeth Taylor's explosive arguments on the set of 'Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?' blurred the line between their real marriage and their on-screen one.
'The Good, the Bad and the Ugly' (1966), directed by Sergio Leone, elevated the Spaghetti Western genre to high art, with Ennio Morricone's score becoming one of the most recognizable in cinema.
Clint Eastwood's Man with No Name trilogy — 'A Fistful of Dollars,' 'For a Few Dollars More,' and 'The Good, the Bad and the Ugly' — was filmed in Spain on minimal budgets and made him a star.
Sergio Leone's Spaghetti Westerns were initially derided by American critics but are now considered among the greatest westerns ever made.
'Bonnie and Clyde' (1967) broke taboos with its graphic violence and sexual content, shocking audiences and helping usher in the New Hollywood era.
Warren Beatty produced and starred in 'Bonnie and Clyde' at age 30, and its surprise success proved young filmmakers could challenge the studio establishment.
'In the Heat of the Night' (1967) won Best Picture and featured Sidney Poitier's famous ad-libbed slap of a wealthy white suspect — one of the most powerful moments in 1960s cinema.
'The Graduate' (1967) made Dustin Hoffman a star and featured the Simon & Garfunkel soundtrack that became iconic of late-1960s disillusionment.

'Guess Who's Coming to Dinner' (1967) was Spencer Tracy's final film; he died only 17 days after shooting completed, and Katharine Hepburn never watched the finished film because seeing him was too painful.
'2001: A Space Odyssey' (1968) was made with no CGI — all of its stunning visual effects were achieved through practical techniques, models, and innovative camera work by Stanley Kubrick.
Stanley Kubrick destroyed nearly all props and models from '2001: A Space Odyssey' after filming to prevent them from being reused in inferior productions.
'Planet of the Apes' (1968) featured groundbreaking prosthetic makeup by John Chambers, who later received an honorary Academy Award for his pioneering work.
'Rosemary's Baby' (1968), directed by Roman Polanski, was one of the first horror films to receive mainstream critical acclaim and major studio backing.
'Night of the Living Dead' (1968) was made for just $114,000 and created the modern zombie genre, though George Romero accidentally lost the copyright through a title change error.
'Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid' (1969) paired Paul Newman and Robert Redford, creating one of cinema's greatest buddy pairings and winning four Oscars.
The Production Code was fully abandoned in 1968, replaced by the MPAA rating system (G, M, R, X) that evolved into today's G, PG, PG-13, R, NC-17 ratings.
Gregory Peck once returned an Oscar statuette mistakenly delivered to him that was intended for a different Gregory — showing the kind of integrity audiences associated with his on-screen persona.
The original 'Phantom of the Opera' (1925) featured Lon Chaney's terrifying self-designed makeup that reportedly caused audience members to faint during early screenings.
Early film studios were built with glass roofs and walls to let in natural light for filming, as artificial lighting technology was not yet advanced enough.
Thomas Edison's Black Maria, built in 1893 in West Orange, New Jersey, was the first movie studio — it rotated on a circular track to follow the sun for lighting.

Hollywood became the center of American filmmaking partly because its reliable sunshine provided consistent natural lighting and its distance from the East Coast helped filmmakers evade Edison's patent enforcement agents.
The star system of the 1910s-1920s began when audiences started recognizing and requesting specific performers, transforming anonymous players into celebrities with enormous drawing power.
Early movie palaces of the 1920s and 1930s were lavish theaters seating thousands, with ornate lobbies, pipe organs, and uniformed ushers — designed to make moviegoing a glamorous event.
'Intolerance' (1916) by D.W. Griffith featured the largest set ever built at the time — a recreation of ancient Babylon over 300 feet tall and a mile deep.

Oscar statuettes were originally priced at $1 each in 1929; winners today must sign an agreement that they or their heirs will offer the statuette back to the Academy for $1 before selling it elsewhere.
The term 'Oscar' for the Academy Award may have originated with Academy librarian Margaret Herrick, who reportedly said the statuette looked like her Uncle Oscar.
'Duck Soup' (1933) was a box office flop and led to the Marx Brothers leaving Paramount, but it's now considered one of the greatest comedies ever made and a brilliant anti-war satire.
The Busby Berkeley musicals of the 1930s featured elaborate kaleidoscopic overhead shots of precision dancers, creating geometric patterns impossible to see from a theater seat.
Busby Berkeley directed dance numbers with up to 100 dancers, using innovative camera angles and editing to create psychedelic-before-psychedelia visual spectacles.

'Top Hat' (1935) is considered the finest Astaire-Rogers musical, featuring Irving Berlin songs including 'Cheek to Cheek,' during which Ginger Rogers's feather dress shed continuously, annoying Astaire.
The phrase 'Ginger Rogers did everything Fred Astaire did, backwards and in high heels' has become a famous feminist observation about undervalued effort.
Boris Karloff's real name was William Henry Pratt, and he was a gentle, well-educated Englishman — the complete opposite of the monsters he famously portrayed.
'Bride of Frankenstein' (1935) is often considered superior to the original, with director James Whale infusing it with dark humor and subversive themes.
Early Technicolor films required massive, heavy cameras that used three strips of film simultaneously, making filming slower and more expensive.
Edith Head won a record eight Academy Awards for Best Costume Design, dressing stars from Mae West to Paul Newman over a career spanning five decades.
Max Steiner composed the score for 'King Kong' (1933) and is often credited with creating the modern film score, demonstrating how music could enhance dramatic storytelling.
Bernard Herrmann's partnership with Alfred Hitchcock produced some of cinema's most iconic scores, including the shrieking violins of 'Psycho' and the haunting 'Vertigo' theme.
In the early days of cinema, films were often hand-tinted frame by frame to add color, a painstaking process that required teams of workers painting each individual frame.
'Pandora's Box' (1929) starring Louise Brooks was poorly received upon release but was rediscovered in the 1950s and is now considered a masterpiece of silent cinema.

The 'Kuleshov Effect,' discovered by Soviet filmmaker Lev Kuleshov in the 1910s-1920s, demonstrated that audiences derive meaning from the juxtaposition of shots rather than individual images — a foundational principle of film editing.
Soviet montage theory, developed by filmmakers like Sergei Eisenstein, proposed that the collision of two shots creates meaning greater than either shot alone.
Sergei Eisenstein's 'Battleship Potemkin' (1925) features the Odessa Steps massacre sequence, one of the most studied and imitated scenes in all of cinema.
'Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans' (1927) by F.W. Murnau won the first (and only) Academy Award for 'Unique and Artistic Production,' a category discontinued after the inaugural ceremony.
Carl Theodor Dreyer's 'The Passion of Joan of Arc' (1928) is celebrated for its extreme close-ups of Renée Falconetti's face, often called the greatest performance in silent cinema.
'Un Chien Andalou' (1929) by Luis Buñuel and Salvador Dalí shocked audiences with its surrealist imagery, including the infamous eye-slicing scene using a dead calf's eye.
Jean Renoir's 'The Rules of the Game' (1939) was booed at its premiere and banned by the French government, but is now regularly voted one of the greatest films ever made.
'The Blue Angel' (1930) made Marlene Dietrich an international star and established her persona as the alluring, world-weary femme fatale.
Marlene Dietrich and Greta Garbo were fierce rivals in the 1930s, with their studios carefully positioning them against each other in competing glamour roles.
Warner Bros. was the first studio to embrace sound technology, partly out of desperation — they were the smallest of the major studios and needed a competitive advantage.
The Fox Movietone newsreel system, which recorded sound directly onto film, was used to capture Charles Lindbergh's departure from New York for his transatlantic flight in 1927.
Early 'talkies' were often static and stagy because the microphones couldn't move and cameras had to be placed in soundproof booths, temporarily undoing years of visual innovation in silent film.
The musical genre exploded with the coming of sound — the first all-talking, all-singing film was 'The Broadway Melody' (1929), which won Best Picture.
'42nd Street' (1933) revitalized the movie musical during the Great Depression with its rags-to-riches backstage storyline and Busby Berkeley choreography.

Child star Jackie Coogan, who starred with Chaplin in 'The Kid' (1921), earned millions but was left with almost nothing by his parents — leading to California's Coogan Law protecting child actors' earnings.
The Hays Code specifically prohibited showing a crime being committed in detail that could serve as instruction, criminals going unpunished, or any mockery of the clergy.
Joseph Breen, who enforced the Hays Code from 1934 to 1954, personally reviewed every studio screenplay and had the power to deny a film a seal of approval, effectively banning it.
Humphrey Bogart initially played villains and gangsters at Warner Bros. before 'The Maltese Falcon' and 'Casablanca' repositioned him as a romantic leading man.
'Yankee Doodle Dandy' (1942) won James Cagney the Best Actor Oscar for playing George M. Cohan, a dramatic shift from the tough-guy gangster roles that made him famous.
'Mrs. Miniver' (1942) was so effective as wartime propaganda that Winston Churchill said it was more valuable to the war effort than a fleet of destroyers.
The 'Why We Fight' documentary series (1942-1945) was directed by Frank Capra for the U.S. Army, turning the 'It's a Wonderful Life' director into a wartime propagandist.
Rosalind Russell's rapid-fire delivery in 'His Girl Friday' (1940) included dialogue spoken at 240 words per minute, some of the fastest in film history.
Howard Hawks directed masterpieces across multiple genres — screwball comedy ('Bringing Up Baby'), film noir ('The Big Sleep'), western ('Rio Bravo'), and war film ('Sergeant York').
Ava Gardner was considered one of the most beautiful women in Hollywood history, and her turbulent marriage to Frank Sinatra inspired some of his greatest torch songs.

Frank Sinatra won the Best Supporting Actor Oscar for 'From Here to Eternity' (1953), reviving a career that had been nearly dead — his casting allegedly inspired the 'horse head' scene in 'The Godfather.'
'Singin' in the Rain' was only a modest hit on initial release in 1952 but has since been recognized as the greatest movie musical ever made.
The first widescreen CinemaScope film, 'The Robe' (1953), was a massive box office success that convinced studios to adopt the wider aspect ratio.
Dorothy Dandridge was not only the first Black woman nominated for Best Actress but also the first to appear on the cover of Life magazine.
Kim Stanley, considered one of the greatest stage actresses of the 20th century, made very few films, and her legendary Broadway performances exist mostly in memory rather than on film.
Sophia Loren won the Academy Award for Best Actress for 'Two Women' (1961), becoming the first performer to win an Oscar for a non-English language performance.
German-born director Fritz Lang fled Nazi Germany after Joseph Goebbels, impressed by 'Metropolis,' offered him a position heading the German film industry.
Fritz Lang's 'M' (1931) was one of the first sound films to use audio as a narrative device — the killer whistles 'In the Hall of the Mountain King,' allowing witnesses to identify him by sound.
Michael Curtiz, who directed 'Casablanca,' was known for his mangled English, once famously telling someone to 'bring on the empty horses' — a phrase David Niven later used as a book title.
The film vault fire at Fox Studios in 1937 destroyed all the studio's pre-1935 silent films and many early talkies, an incalculable cultural loss.
Technicolor consultant Natalie Kalmus had approval power over every Technicolor film's use of color and would reject scenes she felt used color 'inartistically.'
Jack Warner once called writers 'schmucks with Underwoods' (Underwood was a typewriter brand), reflecting the studios' dismissive attitude toward screenwriters during the Golden Age.
'Key Largo' (1948) was the last of four films Humphrey Bogart and Lauren Bacall made together, completing one of cinema's most beloved on-and-off-screen partnerships.
'Cat on a Hot Tin Roof' (1958) was heavily censored to remove the play's references to homosexuality, fundamentally altering Tennessee Williams's original intent.
Paul Newman's ice-blue eyes were so distinctive that they became his trademark — cinematographers often lit him specifically to highlight their color in both black-and-white and color films.
Sophia Loren and Marcello Mastroianni made 14 films together, becoming Italy's most beloved on-screen couple.
The first international film festival was held in Venice in 1932, making it the world's oldest film festival.
The Cannes Film Festival was first held in 1946, created partly as a response to political interference at the Venice festival.
'Les Diaboliques' (1955) by Henri-Georges Clouzot so impressed Hitchcock that he rushed to buy the rights to a similar French novel, which became 'Vertigo.'
At its peak, the Hollywood studio system produced over 500 films per year — modern Hollywood produces fewer than 200 wide-release films annually.
Double features, where theaters showed two films for the price of one, became standard during the Great Depression to attract cash-strapped audiences.

B-movies were made quickly and cheaply by studios to fill the bottom half of double features, yet many B-movie directors — including Anthony Mann and Joseph H. Lewis — created exceptional low-budget film noir.
Tyrone Power was one of 20th Century Fox's biggest stars in the late 1930s and 1940s, starring in swashbucklers and dramas before dying of a heart attack at 44 during filming.
Rita Hayworth's famous pin-up photo from Life magazine was attached to the nuclear bomb tested at Bikini Atoll in 1946, which she found deeply upsetting.
Rita Hayworth's real name was Margarita Carmen Cansino; the studio raised her hairline through electrolysis, dyed her hair red, and changed her name to make her appear less Latina.
'Gilda' (1946) starring Rita Hayworth contained a striptease scene (removing only a glove) that was so suggestive it redefined the limits of on-screen sensuality under the Hays Code.
Lana Turner was reportedly discovered at a soda fountain on Sunset Boulevard at age 16 — though the story may be Hollywood legend, it perfectly captured the dream of instant stardom.
William Holden became one of the biggest stars of the 1950s with films like 'Sunset Boulevard,' 'Stalag 17,' and 'The Bridge on the River Kwai.'
'Rebel Without a Cause' was one of the first films to portray middle-class juvenile delinquency, suggesting that dysfunction wasn't limited to poverty.
Screen legend Barbara Stanwyck never won a competitive Academy Award despite four nominations, though she received an honorary Oscar in 1982.

'The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance' (1962) was John Ford's elegiac meditation on the clash between wilderness and civilization in the American West, featuring the famous line: 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend.'
The French New Wave (Nouvelle Vague) of the late 1950s and 1960s — led by Godard, Truffaut, and others — was directly inspired by and in dialogue with classic Hollywood cinema.
François Truffaut's 'The 400 Blows' (1959) launched the French New Wave and was dedicated to André Bazin, the critic who championed the artistic potential of cinema.
Jean-Luc Godard's 'Breathless' (1960) used jump cuts and handheld camera work that violated every rule of classical Hollywood filmmaking — and changed cinema forever.
The British New Wave films of the early 1960s — 'Saturday Night and Sunday Morning,' 'A Taste of Honey,' 'The Loneliness of the Long Distance Runner' — brought working-class realism to the screen.
'Spartacus' (1960) was notable for Stanley Kubrick taking over direction from Anthony Mann and for its 'I am Spartacus!' scene, where slaves stand to protect their leader's identity.
'Spartacus' featured the most expensive battle scene ever filmed at the time, using 8,500 trained Spanish soldiers as extras.
'The Birds' used over 3,200 trained birds during filming, and Tippi Hedren was actually attacked by real birds in the attic scene, leaving her physically and emotionally exhausted.
Tippi Hedren has alleged that Hitchcock became obsessively controlling during the filming of 'The Birds' and 'Marnie,' effectively blacklisting her when she rejected his advances.
Jack Lemmon and Walter Matthau appeared in 10 films together, beginning with 'The Fortune Cookie' (1966) and creating one of cinema's greatest comic partnerships.
Doris Day was the top box office female star of the late 1950s and early 1960s, known for wholesome romantic comedies, though she was a far more versatile actress than her image suggested.
Rock Hudson and Doris Day's three romantic comedies — 'Pillow Talk' (1959), 'Lover Come Back' (1961), and 'Send Me No Flowers' (1964) — were hugely popular and defined the rom-com genre of the era.
'Psycho' cost only $806,947 to make — Hitchcock financed it himself after Paramount refused — and it grossed over $50 million, making it one of the most profitable films of all time.
Anthony Perkins became so identified with Norman Bates after 'Psycho' that it effectively typecast him for the rest of his career.
The 'wrong man' theme appears in many Hitchcock films — 'The 39 Steps,' 'North by Northwest,' 'The Wrong Man' — reflecting Hitchcock's personal fear of being falsely accused.
Before 'Psycho,' Hitchcock produced a television anthology series, 'Alfred Hitchcock Presents' (1955-1965), which made him one of the most recognized figures in America.
The Panavision camera system, introduced in the late 1950s, became the industry standard by the 1960s, replacing CinemaScope as the preferred widescreen format.
Saul Bass designed some of cinema's most iconic title sequences, including those for 'Vertigo,' 'North by Northwest,' 'Psycho,' and 'Anatomy of a Murder.'
In classic Hollywood, most stunts were performed by the actors themselves or by uncredited stunt doubles — the profession of stunt coordination didn't become formalized until the 1960s.
Foley artists, named after sound effects pioneer Jack Foley, created all the incidental sounds in Golden Age films — from footsteps to breaking glass — in post-production.
The Academy Awards show was first televised in 1953 on NBC, with Bob Hope as host, bringing the glamour of Hollywood into millions of American living rooms.
